data.title: Hallador Energy plunges after datacenter developer terminates exclusivity deal
data.description:
Hallador Energy (NASDAQ:HNRG) -19.1% pre-market Friday after disclosing its agreement with a datacenter developer that granted exclusivity in a potential power supply deal was terminated by the counterparty.

"The parties continue to discuss an additional exclusivity period and continue in non-exclusive discussions," Hallador (NASDAQ:HNRG) said, adding that it is evaluating opportunities with other parties and believes "the competitive tension introduced by these third-party negotiations enhances its positioning to execute a strategic transaction."

Hallador (HNRG) signed the agreement with "a leading global datacenter developer" in January; if finalized, the contract would help sell most of the company's produced energy and capacity at prices higher than the forward curve for more than a decade.

"Given the inherent complexity of these multi-party agreements, it is uncertain that we will finalize terms before the exclusivity expires," Hallador (HNRG) CEO Brent Bilsland had said earlier this month.

data.title: Stock index futures fall sharply, yields slip as Trump threatens tariffs on Apple, EU
data.description:
Stock index futures fell sharply on Friday, while yields slipped, as President Donald Trump said tariffs must be paid on iPhones not made in the U.S. and also threatened a 50% tariff on the European Union.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) -1.3%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) -1.7%, and Dow futures (INDU) -1.1%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 4 basis points to 4.49%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.94%.

Trump, through his social media platform Truth Social, said he is recommending a straight 50% tariff on the European Union, commencing on June 1, 2025.

Apple (AAPL) shares fell 2.5% in premarket trading on Friday after Trump said the tech giant would have to pay 25% tariffs on iPhones not made in the U.S.

Wall Street's major averages ended largely flat on Thursday, while a bruising bond sell-off from the previous session eased up. The focus remained on Trump's “One Big Beautiful” tax bill, which was narrowly passed by the U.S. House.

U.S. Treasury yields stabilized on Thursday after soaring on Wednesday, as traders had dumped bonds amid concerns over the new tax bill's effects on the ballooning U.S. fiscal deficit.

“After a heavy selloff on Wednesday, markets began to stabilize again over the last 24 hours, despite investors’ ongoing fears about the fiscal situation,” Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

One factor helping U.S. assets to stabilize was some stronger U.S. economic data, which continued to point away from a recession, Allen added.

Administration comments suggest Trump is willing to increase taxes on U.S. consumers of EU-made products if the EU does not make unilateral concessions on tariffs, UBS' Paul Donovan said.

“Markets are unlikely to place too much weight on these threats given the president’s tendency to rapidly retreat on such issues, but the situation does (again) increase policy uncertainty,” Donovan added.

The economic calendar is quiet today with mainly the April new home sales on tap. Economists on average expect it to fall to 694K.

U.S. markets will be closed on Monday for Memorial Day.

data.title: Apple suppliers see red after Trump says tech giant must pay tariffs on iPhones not made in US
data.description:
Stocks of several suppliers of Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) fell premarket on Friday after President Donald Trump said the tech giant would have to pay 25% tariffs on iPhones not made in the U.S.

Shares of Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) fell about 2% each, while Qorvo (NASDAQ:QRVO) and Skyworks Solutions (NASDAQ:SWKS) declined about 3% each. Qorvo and Skyworks make antenna tuners and radio frequency, or RF, related products for Apple. Meanwhile, Broadcom and Qualcomm provide chip related solutions used for the iPhone.

"I have long ago informed Tim Cook of Apple that I expect their iPhone’s that will be sold in the United  States of America will be manufactured and built in the United States, not India, or anyplace else. If that is not the case, a Tariff of at least 25% must be paid by Apple to the U.S." Trump said in a post on social media platform Truth Social on Friday. 

Earlier this month, Trump had urged Cook to stop building facilities in India.

Apple's stock declined about 3% premarket.

Jabil, which is a contract manufacturer of electrical components, saw its stock dip about 1% premarket on Friday. However, shares of Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF), also known as Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF), rose about 1% in Taiwan. Earlier on Friday it was reported that Foxconn is among potential bidders for UTAC Holdings in a transaction that could value the Singapore-based semiconductor assembly and testing company at about $3B. 

Shares of Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI), another Apple supplier, declined about 3%.

data.title: Xiaomi pledges $28 billion for core tech R&D over next five years
data.description:
China's Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) (OTCPK:XIACF) to inject 200 billion yuan ($27.7 billion) in core technology research and development over the next five years, founder and CEO Lei Jun said at an event in Beijing.

Xiaomi also revealed its first electric SUV at the event, marking its entry into the electric vehicle market.

data.title: Multi Ways Holdings faces NYSE non-compliance over delayed annual report filing
data.description:
• Multi Ways Holdings (NYSE:MWG) received notification from New York Stock Exchange that, as a result of not having timely filed its annual report for 2024, MWG is not in compliance with NYSE American listing standards.
  • The filing delay is due to the additional time needed to finalize financial disclosures with its independent auditors.
  • MWG aims to file the 2024 annual report by May 30, 2025.
  • The NYSE will monitor the situation during a six-month Initial Cure Period; failure to comply may lead to suspension and delisting procedures.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Talc should be avoided in lieu of alternatives, scientific experts tell FDA
data.description:
A panel of scientific experts brought together by the FDA this week urged the agency to scale back the use of the naturally occurring mineral talc present in personal care products, cosmetics, and drugs, and use alternatives instead.

Talc has long been thought to potentially contribute to the development of ovarian cancer. The WHO's International Agency for Research on Cancer found that talc is "probably carcinogenic to humans."

The May 20 roundtable of experts called on the FDA to study and adopt new standards that would end up banning talc in consumer products.

"It's clear (talc) is carcinogenic and inflammatory and there’s very little doubt about this,” said George Tidmarsh, a pediatric neonatologist and professor at Stanford University, who was one of the panelists said. "In my opinion, it's not a matter of if talc should be removed from the U.S. market, it's a matter of how and when."

FDA Commissioner Marty Makary noted that talc is present in candy and food. He added it is also in four of the 10 most-prescribed medicines in the U.S.: Lipitor, Synthroid, Prilosec, and Neurontin.

"If you've ever unwrapped a piece of gum, that white powder that keeps it from sticking to the wrapper — that's talc that you're putting in your mouth that we now know is carcinogenic,” Tidmarsh said.

Representatives from the food industry were not on the panel, Chemical & Engineering News reported.

Potential alternatives to talc include magnesium stearate, calcium stearate, and colloidal silicon dioxide, noted Steven Pfeiffer, an organic chemist at privately held Mirador Therapeutics, adding that the substances are safe, cheap, and readily available. 

For years, Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) has contended with lawsuits arguing that the healthcare giant's talc-based products led to ovarian cancer. In April, J&J's third attempt to resolve these cases by creating a division that would declare bankruptcy, was rejected by a judge.

In 2023, J&J ended the sale of talc-based powders worldwide. It contains cornstarch instead.

data.title: GSK wins EU backing for multiple myeloma drug, Blenrep
data.description:
GSK (NYSE:GSK) announced Friday that an expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency (EMA), endorsed its antibody-drug conjugate Blenrep as a late-line option and as part of a combination regimen for adults with multiple myeloma, a type of blood cancer.

Issuing a positive opinion, EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has recommended approval of Blenrep for adults with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma, the British drugmaker said.

The CHMP backed the use of Blenrep as part of two combination regimens: one with bortezomib plus dexamethasone (BVd) and another with pomalidomide plus dexamethasone (BPd) in those who have received at least one prior therapy.

The recommendations will next be reviewed by the European Commission (EC) from which a final decision on the drug’s marketing authorization is expected in Q3 2025, GSK said.

In 2024, the company pulled Blenrep from the European market after the regulators declined to renew the conditional marketing authorization granted for the medicine in R/R multiple myeloma over efficacy concerns. However, months later GSK renewed its bid to market the drug, and the EMA accepted its marketing authorization application in July 2024.

data.title: Ashford Hospitality to sell Houston hotel for $27M
data.description:
Ashford Hospitality Trust (NYSE:AHT) agreed to sell the Hilton Houston NASA Clear Lake for $27.0M, a transaction that will help the REIT reduce some debt and capital spending, the company said Friday.

The hotel REIT has been opportunistically selling some properties to transform the company. It has additional assets on the market at various stages of the sale process, it added.

"This sale will deleverage our Morgan Stanley 17 Pool loan, and result in significant capital expenditure savings," Ashford Hospitality (NYSE:AHT) President and CEO Stephen Zsigray said.

Ashford Hospitality (AHT) stock rose 5.8% in Friday premarket trading.

When adjusted for anticipated capital expenditures on the property, the sale price represents a  3.2% capitalization rate on net operating income, or 23.6x hotel EBITDA for the 12 months ended April 30, 2025. Excluding the anticipated capital spending, the price represents a 5.0% capitalization rate on net operating income, or 15.2x hotel EBITDA for the 12 months ended April 30.
